results.possibleIdentity: Everett Aison is a multi-disciplinary artist, graphic designer, filmmaker, author, and educator who was prominent in the New York art and design scene starting in the late 1950s. He is most widely recognized as the co-founder of the film department at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York, which he established in the early 1960s and led for nearly 40 years. As a graphic designer, Aison created iconic minimalist movie posters, most notably the 1961 U.S. poster for Akira Kurosawa’s 'Yojimbo' and Roman Polanski’s 'Knife in the Water'. His work in film includes designing title sequences for movies such as 'Black Like Me' and 'A Bowl of Cherries', as well as directing his own short films like 'Post No Bills' (1968) and 'So Much in Common' (early 1970s). Aison was also active as an illustrator and author of children's books, including 'The Lion' (1964) and 'Arthur' (with Rhoda Levine). In 2006, he published a novel titled 'Artrage', which satirized the contemporary art world. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he was a key figure at SVA, an institution that frequently overlapped with the experimental art movements of the time, including the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle in New York.
